facts_disclosed: Moved to Chapinero — security concerns, doorman, cameras

turns.content: ugh I hate unpacking. everything smells like cardboard and I can't find the coffee maker
Moving is the worst! Nothing feels right until you can at least get coffee going. Did you just move to a new place?

yeah. chapinero. we moved last weekend and I'm still surrounded by boxes. the building has a portero downstairs, security cameras on every floor, key fob for the elevator. lucía picked it specifically for all of that
That sounds like a building with serious security infrastructure. It must be a relief for Lucía knowing those measures are in place. How are you feeling about the new neighborhood?

honestly? I hate it. I mean chapinero is fine, the restaurants are good, there's a ciclorruta nearby. but it's not home. I was in la candelaria before and I loved that neighborhood with everything I had. the noise, the murals, the señora downstairs who sold empanadas at 6am. I knew every street, every corner, every crack in the sidewalk

That kind of deep connection to a neighborhood is hard to replicate. La Candelaria has a character that's really unique in Bogotá — the history, the street art, the rawness of it. Chapinero is a different vibe entirely.

It sounds like this wasn't really a choice you made because you wanted something new. Was the move driven more by practical concerns?

practical. yeah. that's one word for it. I had some threats related to my work and lucía basically said we're moving somewhere with real security or she was going to lose her mind worrying. and she was right to push for it. I know that. I'm a journalist in colombia, I'm not naive about what can happen

I'm sorry you're dealing with that. Threats are something no one should have to normalize, and Lucía's concern makes complete sense. Having a building with a doorman, cameras, and controlled access does meaningfully reduce risk.

It's also okay to grieve the place you left. Being safe and being sad about it aren't contradictory.

that's exactly it. both things at the same time. I miss la candelaria every single day and I also want to stay alive so here I am in chapinero with my key fob and my portero who calls me doctora even though I've told him three times to just say valentina

Ha — the "doctora" thing is never going away, that's just how it works. You might as well accept that one.

Give it some time. You won't love Chapinero the way you loved La Candelaria, and that's fine. But you'll find your routines — a coffee spot, a walking route, maybe a new empanada source. It won't replace what you had, but it'll become yours in a different way.

yeah maybe. lucía already found a bakery she likes two blocks away and she's been annoyingly optimistic about the whole thing. she keeps saying the light is better in the new apartment which is true but I refuse to admit it

The light thing is significant though — especially if you're someone who works late and needs the daytime hours to feel human. And Lucía finding her bakery already is a good sign. She's nesting.

Maybe start with one thing you actually like about the new place, even if it's small. The light counts.

fine. the light is better. the shower has actual water pressure which is new for me. and the portero noticed I come home late and now he saves me a tinto from his thermos which is kind of sweet actually

See? A portero who saves you tinto is basically the Chapinero version of the empanada señora. You're already building the new version of home.

Now go find that coffee maker — it's probably in the box you labeled least logically.

lucía labeled the boxes not me so it's probably in the one marked 'bathroom miscellaneous.' okay I'm going to dig through these. thanks for letting me complain parce. I know it's not a real problem in the grand scheme of things

It is a real thing though — leaving a place you loved isn't trivial, even when the reason is important. Take your time settling in. And tell the portero his tinto service is appreciated. That man is doing God's work.
